rho: Efficiency may appear as more miles or better utilization rather than reduced headcount.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Specialized Freight (except Used Goods) Trucking, Long-Distance

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Commercial shippers buy repeat or contracted long-distance transport of cargo requiring flatbeds, tankers, refrigerated trailers, auto carriers, livestock or other specialized equipment, priced by load, mile, ton, lane, dedicated capacity, or negotiated contract with fuel and accessorial adjustments.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive private fleets, pure brokers and dispatch-only firms, general freight carriers, used-goods movers, independent drivers without a transferable operating organization, and financing or shell entities.
lens.included_activities: Independent carriers transporting specialized freight other than used household and office goods between metropolitan areas, including cross-border North American routes, with their own operating organization and responsibility for vehicles, cargo, safety, and service.

a.bridge: GAO and DOT evidence on driving automation in broad long-haul trucking is combined with occupational GenAI evidence for clerical work. Highway driving receives partial exposure where an automated system could perform bounded route segments; dispatch, routing, pricing, communications, compliance records, billing, and administration receive higher exposure; loading, securement, inspection, maintenance, emergencies, and cargo-specific handling receive low exposure. The evidence is not wage-weighted for NAICS 484230 and general long-haul routes are easier than many specialized loads, so applying it directly would likely overstate exposure.
caveats: Autonomous-driving capability is not equivalent to safe or lawful labor substitution.
Cargo niches differ sharply in securement, inspection, temperature, hazardous-material, animal-welfare, and border requirements.
The available specialized-freight occupational evidence is broader than this six-digit industry.
a.change_evidence: A current NAICS 484230 wage-weighted staffing and task study showing driver time by operational-design-domain-compatible highway miles, terminal and customer work, cargo handling, and office functions would materially revise the range.
a.rationale: Unlike local trucking, a material share of driver time occurs on access-controlled highways that automation developers target, expanding exposure beyond the back office. Specialized cargo still requires substantial human pre-trip, securement, loading-interface, inspection, border, customer-site, and exception work, while office workflows offer nearer-term AI substitution.

d5.bridge: BTS/FHWA forecasts total US freight tonnage to grow 50% from 2020 to 2050, with trucks carrying 65% of tonnage, and BLS projects heavy and tractor-trailer driver employment to grow 4% from 2024 to 2034. These broad national freight and occupation trends anchor modest five-year growth, then are widened because they include general freight, other modes, local trips, and industries outside specialized long-distance hauling. Commodity composition could make the proxy overstate or understate this niche.
caveats: Freight tonnage is not constant-quality specialized trucking service quantity.
The national driver forecast spans industries and does not isolate demand from labor supply or productivity.
Cross-border, energy, agricultural, and construction cycles can dominate particular fleets.
d5.change_evidence: FAF truck forecasts filtered to long-distance lanes and relevant commodity classes, paired with a constant-price load, ton-mile, and service-quality panel for NAICS 484230, would materially improve the ratio.
d5.rationale: Autos, refrigerated goods, farm products, bulk liquids, forest products, machinery, and other specialized cargo must continue moving between regions. Aggregate freight and driver outlooks support modest real growth, while commodity cycles, energy transition, trade policy, nearshoring, and modal shifts create substantial dispersion.

caveats: The frozen count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms around the EBITDA band.
Census establishments are not firms, and FMCSA registrations are not proof of an active transferable enterprise.
Transferability varies by whether value resides in contracts, permits, equipment, drivers, or the owner relationship.
e.change_evidence: A representative audit of the frozen 1,036-firm population using FMCSA census files, operating authority, vehicle and driver counts, customer concentration, equipment ownership, management depth, and cargo permits would directly estimate eligibility.
e.rationale: The industry definition describes outsourced operating carriers, so a majority of LMM firms should fit. Ineligibility arises from captive or misclassified fleets, broker shells, single-driver jobs without transferable organization, firms with fragile authority or compliance, and operations whose customer concentration or owner dependence prevents a credible control transfer.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $12,466.3M / sectoral output (T30) $58,180.5M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1558 (margins.json[484]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Trucking EBITDA/Sales 15.58%); revenue/employee $294,919 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 484230); class <5: 7,400 firms, avg revenue/firm $555,854 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $86,602 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,036 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,187,228 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $340,770 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 638 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,808,119 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $749,105 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 770 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,259,943 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,065,899 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 266 firms, avg revenue/firm $39,730,568 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,190,022 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 182 firms, avg revenue/firm $115,274,203 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,959,721 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,036

o.bridge: GAO's broad long-haul automation evidence presents both driver-reducing and operator-retaining scenarios and finds widespread deployment subject to technological and acceptance constraints. The quantity here is narrower: whether demand still needs an accountable carrier, not whether a driver remains in every cab. Even driver-out highway segments require an entity for vehicles, cargo custody, permits, insurance, remote supervision, handoffs, and exceptions; using driver displacement as operator elimination would therefore bias the loss upward.
caveats: The estimate is about the accountable operating firm rather than driver employment.
Autonomous-network operators may still satisfy the lens even if traditional carriers lose the load.
Regulatory approvals and insurance availability can change quickly and unevenly by state.
o.change_evidence: Scaled driver-out shipment data that identifies whether fleets are independent carriers, shipper-owned, or platform-controlled, plus customer procurement evidence on carrier-of-record responsibility by cargo niche, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Software, brokers, and autonomous tractors can change who drives or coordinates a load, but specialized shippers still need a responsible operating carrier for physical equipment, cargo integrity, legal compliance, insurance, and recovery from failures. A minority of standardized lanes may shift to autonomous networks or shipper self-operation within five years.

caveats: Producer-price indexes show received-price movement, not causal pass-through of a specific productivity gain.
Pricing power varies substantially across hazardous materials, refrigeration, autos, bulk products, livestock, and oversize loads.
The estimate excludes implementation costs and demand effects.
q.change_evidence: Contract and load panels connecting rate structure, broker involvement, bid cadence, lane density, cargo niche, realized price, and post-automation cost changes would establish the discounted retained share.
q.rationale: Fixed lane, dedicated-capacity, and term contracts can preserve savings until renewal, while scarce permits, equipment, service quality, or cargo expertise can sustain differentiation. However, sophisticated shippers, brokers, lane benchmarks, spot capacity, rebids, and fuel or accessorial formulas expose carrier cost improvements to price competition, causing substantial five-year sharing.

caveats: Government automation assessments predate some recent technical advances.
Pilot miles and announced routes do not establish scaled driver removal.
Efficiency may appear as more miles or better utilization rather than reduced headcount.
rho.change_evidence: Audited commercial-deployment data by cargo type showing driver-out miles, interventions, safety performance, insurance terms, operating jurisdictions, and realized labor hours per load, together with carrier back-office adoption cohorts, would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Back-office AI can be implemented through existing transportation-management, telematics, and ELD workflows, but driving substitution has much lower five-year implementation because approved routes, vehicle retrofits, weather, cargo-specific safety, state and federal rules, insurance, cybersecurity, public acceptance, remote assistance, and first- and last-mile handoffs constrain deployment. Weighting these together produces implementation well below raw exposure.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding employer-business owners across all US industries were age 55 or older. This is translated to a far smaller five-year qualifying control-transfer probability after excluding continued ownership, closures, asset-only liquidations, and internal changes. The source is not trucking-specific and age is only a pressure indicator, so a direct use would bias transfer probability upward.
caveats: The owner-age evidence covers all industries and reference year 2018.
An operating-authority or equipment transfer may not be a qualifying company control transfer.
Private trucking transactions are incompletely reported.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of NAICS-coded private transactions, Secretary of State control changes, FMCSA authority and insurance continuity, equipment-finance events, and brokered listings matched to an operating-carrier roster would materially revise this probability.
s5.rationale: A fragmented carrier base and aging owners support succession supply, but volatile freight markets, equipment debt, insurance history, compliance quality, and owner dependence can cause shutdowns or asset sales rather than transferable control events.

principal_driver: Repeat, document-intensive freight operations offer back-office automation today and selective highway-driving automation over time while cargo remains physically anchored.
principal_weakness: The largest theoretical exposure is driving, but specialized cargo and deployment constraints make its five-year implementation and retained economics unusually uncertain.
